title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given a sequence of n integers, find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
input_spec: The first line contains an integer n (1 <= n <= 2 * 10^5). The second line contains n integers a_1, ..., a_n (-10^9 <= a_i <= 10^9).
output_spec: Print the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

routes.name: Patience sorting (O(n log n))
routes.description: Maintain an array of smallest tail elements. Use binary search to find the correct position for each new element.

observations.title: Tails array invariant
observations.description: Maintain array tails[] where tails[i] = smallest possible tail element of an increasing subsequence of length i+1.

hints.nudge: Instead of tracking entire subsequences, what minimal information per length do you need?
hints.question: For each possible LIS length, what single value would be most useful to remember?
hints.formal: tails[i] = smallest element that ends an increasing subsequence of length i+1. This array is always sorted.

observations.title: Binary search for position
observations.description: For each new element, binary search tails[] for the leftmost value >= element. Replace it, or append if element is larger than all.

hints.nudge: Since tails is sorted, how can you efficiently find where a new element fits?
hints.question: When you see a new element x, you want to find the first tail >= x. What algorithm finds this in a sorted array?
hints.formal: Use bisect_left (lower_bound) to find position p. If p == len(tails), append x; else tails[p] = x. This is O(log n) per element.

observations.title: Answer is length of tails
observations.description: After processing all elements, the length of the tails array equals the LIS length.

hints.nudge: After processing all elements, where is the answer?
hints.question: What does the length of the tails array represent?
hints.formal: len(tails) after processing all elements = LIS length. Note: tails itself is NOT the LIS, just its tails.

routes.name: O(n^2) DP
routes.description: Classic quadratic DP where dp[i] = length of LIS ending at index i.

observations.title: DP state: LIS ending at i
observations.description: dp[i] = length of longest increasing subsequence that ends with a[i].

hints.nudge: What if you defined a subproblem based on where the subsequence ends?
hints.question: If dp[i] represents the LIS length ending at position i, how would you compute it?
hints.formal: dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j] for all j < i where a[j] < a[i]). Base: dp[i] = 1.

observations.title: Answer is max of all dp[i]
observations.description: The LIS could end at any position. The answer is max(dp[0], dp[1], ..., dp[n-1]).

hints.nudge: The LIS doesn't have to end at the last element. Where could it end?
hints.question: After computing dp[i] for all i, how do you get the final answer?
hints.formal: Answer = max(dp[i]) for 0 <= i < n. The subsequence can end anywhere.

observations.title: O(n^2) may be too slow for n = 2*10^5
observations.description: This approach is O(n^2). With n up to 2*10^5, it may TLE. Consider the O(n log n) patience sorting approach.

hints.nudge: Check the constraints. Is O(n^2) fast enough?
hints.question: n can be 2*10^5. How many operations is n^2? Is that within the time limit?
hints.formal: n^2 = 4*10^10 operations -- way too slow. You need the O(n log n) approach using binary search on a tails array.
